data.title: Dollar Weakness Boosts International Appeal
data.description:
By Kristy Akullian, CFA

A Case For Going International

International stocks trounced U.S. equity markets in the first half of 2025, in one of the largest performance gaps in decades (Figure 1). While we still believe many U.S. companies are exceptional, and we continue to believe in the growth potential of the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, we see three reasons for U.S. investors to consider increased exposures to international stocks, as detailed below: the dollar’s downturn, concentration risk in U.S. large-cap indexes like the S&P 500, and the value tilt of international equities.

Figure 1: U.S. vs. International performance

The Declining Dollar

The dollar is down nearly 10% year to date.1 Still, we believe it has farther to fall. The dollar has long benefited from a cycle of robust U.S. growth and investment, attracting foreign capital which in turn strengthened the dollar, creating a virtuous cycle. However, we think the dollar’s structural bull market that has persisted since 2014 may be coming to an end as American exceptionalism is challenged. Unpredictable trade policy, a reorientation of global growth, or demand for alternative reserve currencies could all weaken foreign demand for U.S. assets, putting further downward pressure on the dollar.

Over the last decade, a strong dollar has dampened international returns for a U.S.-based investor. Consider Figure 2, where unhedged international developed market returns (MSCI EAFE Index) benefited from currency impacts in just two of the preceding 10 years. Should our expectations for further dollar weakness be realized, U.S. investors could face a sea change in their experience of international investing, where currency becomes a tailwind to bolster returns rather than a headwind to diminish them.

Figure 2: Dollar strength has historically been a drag on international equity returns

U.S. Concentration Risk

The so-called Magnificent Seven stocks have delivered remarkable returns over the past three years, fueled by the promise of AI and the unrivaled ability of U.S. corporations to generate outsized profits.2 Strong performance from the top has also improved the quality attributes of broad indexes, reducing average leverage and increasing profitability metrics.3 In a world of structurally higher interest rates, this continues to underpin our preference for high quality, primarily large cap, U.S. companies.

The flip side to this remarkable performance, however, is a historic rise in concentration: market cap-weighted indices like the S&P 500 are as concentrated as they have ever been. Thirty years ago, the top 20 names in the S&P 500 represented about 28% of the total index. Today, the top 20 names account for over 40%.4

The same phenomenon is happening at a global level as well, where the share of U.S. equities in global indexes has also reached record highs. In 2005, the U.S. made up just 50% of the MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) universe (Figure 3). Twenty years of American exceptionalism later, that number has climbed to 64% (Figure 3).

While an overweight position in U.S. large caps has paid off over the last decade and a half, concentration risk is still a top cited concern amongst investors we speak to. As leadership narrows, headline-level returns are increasingly beholden to a smaller list of constituents and countries, potentially making portfolios riskier overall.

Alongside the tailwind of a declining dollar, we favor international indexes as a diversification tool precisely because they have bucked the concentration trend. Since 2005, the three largest countries in MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index — the U.K., Japan and France — have steadily ceded ground, with their combined weight declining as leadership has broadened out.5

Figure 3.1: Concentration swells in the U.S.

Figure 3.2: While declining elsewhere

As investors look to build more balanced portfolios — with H1’s performance a stark reminder of why — we prefer international equities as potential diversifiers to large-cap U.S. stocks. International equities have delivered both lower volatility and lower correlation relative to the S&P 500 than small caps over the last decade.6 That relationship is emphasized in drawdown periods: since 2010, when the S&P 500 posted negative quarterly returns, small caps underperformed significantly — posting nearly double the losses of developed markets. In fact, since 1999, in months when the S&P 500 was down, small caps were always down by more than international equities.7

In addition to their dimmer diversification prospects, the macro outlook also remains a headwind for small caps. Smaller, riskier companies have tended to perform best when we are coming out of a recession — not when we see U.S. growth slowing in the quarters ahead.8

Factors Favor International Stocks

International markets often have different drivers of risk and return than U.S. ones. One way to quantify these differences is by factor breakdown. The U.S. has long benefitted from its inherent growth tilt, while both developed and emerging markets often exhibit higher exposure to the value factor, with higher dividend and earnings yields than their U.S. counterparts.9 By incorporating international equities into a diversified portfolio, investors may benefit from structural geopolitical or thematic forces, while also offsetting some of the inherent growth bias within their U.S. equity sleeves.

The alpha generated by factor tilting is even greater in international markets than it is in U.S. ones, making factor investing a compelling implementation style for those looking to add exposure. Currently, we favor the quality factor in international markets, which screens for companies that have the earnings stability and cash flows to weather a volatile geopolitical backdrop. The quality factor also exhibits an asymmetric up/down capture profile — meaning it has historically participated to a greater degree on the upside than on the downside.10

Conclusion

Home country bias is a perennial theme among U.S. investor portfolios. The average advisor is overweight U.S. equities, with allocations continually increasing over the past decade. Our analysis of more than 20,000 portfolios showed that, on average, advisors allocate as much as 77% of their stock sleeves to U.S. companies11 — compared to a 64% weighting in MSCI All Country World Index.12

But recent polling data suggests that trend may soon reverse.13 Many advisors see opportunity in international equities: the exposure captured the largest share of bullish sentiment in our most recent poll with 43% considering adds in the next 3-6 months. We believe that increased demand could be a catalyst for a further move higher.

U.S. investors interested in adding to international exposures via the ease and tax-efficiency of exchange traded funds (ETFs) may consider:

  • The iShares Core MSCI Total International Stock ETF (IXUS) provides exposure to a broad range of international developed and emerging market companies. IXUS seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of large-, mid- and small-capitalization non-U.S. equities.
  • The iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF (IDEV) provides low cost exposure to over 20 global developed markets, excluding the U.S. IDEV seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of large-, mid- and small-capitalization developed market equities, excluding the United States.
  • Investors specifically interested in accessing the quality factor in international markets may consider the iShares MSCI Intl Quality Factor ETF (IQLT), which provides exposure to developed international stocks exhibiting positive fundamentals: high return on equity, stable year-over-year earnings growth and low financial leverage.

© 2025 BlackRock, Inc. All rights reserved.

1 Bloomberg, as of June 23, 2025, U.S. Dollar Spot Index.

2 Magnificent 7 stocks representative of Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Tesla. As of June 23, 2025. Specific companies or issuers are mentioned for educational purposes only and should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities. Any companies mentioned do not necessarily represent current or future holdings of any BlackRock products. For actual fund holdings, please visit the respective fund product pages.

3 Source: Morningstar, as of June 23, 2025.

4 Bloomberg. As of June 23, 2025.

Index performance does not reflect any management fees, transaction costs or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Up capture is how much the investment gains when the broad market rises, like the Foreign Large Blend Morningstar Category in this case, and down capture is how much the investment losses when the market was falling.

As such, there may be certain biases present in the data that reflect the advisors who choose to work with BlackRock to analyze their portfolios. data.title: Super Micro Should Get A Super Catalyst (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
My Coverage Of SMCI Stock

I've been covering Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) since January 2024, initially rating the stock as a Buy, and then withdrawing that rating and assigning it again as the market conditions and the firm's situation have changed. Since my most recent bullish article, which came out in May 2025, I argued that SMCI was about to rise like a phoenix from the ashes because it was heavily oversold amid clearly changing market dynamics and upcoming corporate events (such as strategic clearing for Nvidia (NVDA) Blackwell and new Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) solutions), which should have brought some margin recovery opportunities going forward. As we can see now, that bullish rating actually aged well, as SCMI has gone up by about 95% since then.

My Thesis Update

SMCI is set to report for its fiscal Q4 FY2025 on August 5th, and I think it's very likely to get yet another bullish catalyst from it as the firm's addressable market and overall AI-related revenue sources keep getting stronger, thus promising a bullish outlook and likely guidance uplift from the management team. The current consensus estimates seem to be set at a very low bar, so I'm expecting a double beat from SMCI this time. Let me elaborate.

Previewing Super Micro's Q4 2025 Earnings

I discussed SMCI's fiscal Q3 2025 in early May, right the next day after the earnings print, so I'm not going to repeat the whole Q3 review analysis here. Let's just focus on the key things.

SMCI missed on the top line by reporting $4.6 billion in sales (+19% YoY, but ~2.84% below the consensus estimate) - all because customers were delaying orders amid the transition from Nvidia Hopper to Blackwell GPUs. It wasn't an operational breakdown, as the management expected those orders to partially monetize throughout Q4 and Q1 FY2026. Plus, the inventory reserves and all the subsequent margin compression should have eased by the same time.

I assume that the demand for liquid-cooled solutions has been robust in the calendar Q2 of this year - we can see this from both SMCI's plans to invest in Europe ("as demand accelerates"), and also from its peers' success. On July 29th, Accelsius, a leader in direct-to-chip liquid cooling tech, announced that "to meet growing hyperscale and AI infrastructure demand," they've doubled their product portfolio. We know that SMCI has been first to market with B200 HGX and GB200 NVL72 systems, and it has been preparing for B300/GB300 launches this summer, so with $3.9B inventory and 81 days of inventory on hand reported in Q3, we should see a major shipment ramp in fiscal Q4 and Q1 FY2026.

Speaking of European expansion, Super Micro launched over 30 new Blackwell-based AI solutions [as of June 2025], thus reducing the deployment timeline from 12-18 months to just 3 months, according to the source. But Europe is far from the only growth region for the firm: they got into a $20 billion multi-year partnership with Saudi DataVolt "to deliver ultra-dense GPU platforms for hyperscale AI campuses in the UK, Saudi Arabia, and the U.S." Also, they launched a DCBBS with second-gen liquid cooling (DLC-2), which should theoretically increase the power and water savings by 40% at a 30% lower TCO. The product offering of SMCI seems to be a top-notch one on the market - otherwise, it wouldn't have expanded its presence on the branded AI server market to ~31%.

Super Micro owns 9% of the AI platform market and 31% of the market from branded suppliers and Leopold [Raymond James' analyst] said the company should continue to gain share, even if the results are lumpy.

Source: Seeking Alpha News, Oakoff's notes added.

Regarding the upcoming Q4 print, I should note that the management sounded quite confident, expecting the higher end of their initial revenue guidance.

For fiscal year 2025 ending June 30, 2025, based on the Q4 guidance above, we are expecting revenues of $21.8 billion to $22.6 billion [so, subtracting the other 3 quarters' sales figures from the midpoint of this range, we get a figure of ~$5.98 billion for Q4]

<...>

I am confident that we will close the fiscal year on a strong note. Given the current conditions, we anticipate Q4 revenues of at least $6 billion and will resume providing a broader forecast range once we have more clear visibility.

Source: Charles Liang's words, Q3 earnings call in May 2025, Oakoff's notes added.

Interestingly, the market expects a much lower figure for Q4, setting the bar ~1.2% below the midpoint:

The same thing is true regarding Q4 EPS. And I would have understood the origin of this negativity if it were coming from January or February, but it mostly came from just 3 months ago. See the quarterly EPS estimates revisions below:

In my view, the fiscal Q3 was just a reset quarter for Super Micro, and its rising order backlog, the overall expansion of manufacturing capacity across the globe (secured by well-timed financing in June 2025), and also a series of important partnerships, say a lot about what should come next. But the market seems to simply extrapolate the negatives of the past to the future quarters, thus making seemingly unrealistic assumptions (in other words, their assumptions seem too bearish amid the AI market's strength in recent months). All this creates a fertile soil for SMCI's likely solid double-beating of its upcoming Q4 2025.

Super Micro's Valuation Update

As the stock got hit hard by a variety of different things, its valuation premium completely evaporated by the end of 2024, and SMCI entered this year with a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of just ~10-12x. Now, after a little relief rally, it's trading at a 16x FWD EV/EBITDA ratio, which is still below what it used to trade at last year, according to YCharts.

The same is true for its P/E ratio:

I believe that SMCI should trade at ~25x [at least] by the end of calendar 2025, once the company shows that its margins do reverse, and the order book does get monetized.

At the higher end of analysts' consensus estimates for FY2026 EPS [$3.25], SMCI's price target arrives at $81.25/share, which is >30% above the current stock price.

Risks And Your Takeaway

I admit that I may be wrong on multiple sides of my thesis, primarily on my expectation that the Q4 print will play as a bullish catalyst for SMCI's future price action and recovery continuation. The bar for beating is set low indeed, but there are past examples that might justify the cautious view of most analysts on the Street right now. Also, I may be wrong in my price target calculations as I'm basing it on the higher end of the FY2026 EPS consensus range. At the lower end [$1.97], the PT would drop to $49.25 at 25x earnings, so that would give us a downside potential of ~21%. So, the current average price target from Wall Street analysts of $45.38 may be closer to reality than what I'm coming up with in my article.

Despite the above risks, I think SMCI's recovery rally isn't done yet. The odds of its upcoming Q4 print being stronger than what's currently expected about it are actually quite high right now, in my understanding.

I think SMCI will beat Q4 consensus estimates, and I hope it'll guide to a higher Q1/Q2 thanks to the ongoing Blackwell ramp and DCBBS rollout. If so, this will serve as a strong bullish catalyst, which can send the stock to the next resistance level of $66-70/share:

I keep my Buy rating unchanged for now (at least until I see real trouble).

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: ClearBridge Sustainability Leaders Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Despite the push to enact new tariff structures and subsequent trade discussions, consumer sentiment deterioration, and intensifying geopolitical conflicts, U.S. equities rose significantly. The benchmark Russell 3000 Index advanced 11.0%.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in financials, communication services and materials sectors and a lack of energy holdings contributed to relative results.
  • Detractors: Stock selection in the health care and IT sectors, a health care overweight and a communication services underweight detracted.
  • Outlook: While the U.S. economy faces several open questions, we are still not seeing a recession as the base case. Along with interest rates and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, how the consumer reacts to macro pressures will be a key driver of the economy for the rest of the year.

Performance Review

  • In a challenging environment ultimately defined by a major rally, the Fund outperformed the benchmark during the quarter. This represents a significant stabilization in performance following several disappointing quarters.
  • The Fund's defensive positioning entering the quarter drove outperformance in April given significant downside volatility. We observed significant declines across many sectors, even for stocks not particularly economically sensitive or exposed to tariffs. At the same time, some of the more defensive names in the portfolio held up well or even rallied through the volatile period.
  • Key financials contributors for the quarter included JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America and Morgan Stanley, all of which were helped by disciplined execution, resilient consumer spending and credit, reduced tariff fears and signs of increasing capital markets activity. The largest banks are increasingly seeing benefits of scale, benefiting JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America in particular.
  • In communication services, new position Netflix (NFLX) was a leading contributor; the company is accelerating revenues with broadening price hikes, while its new ad technology, which gives advertisers new ways to buy ads and target viewers, supports a further jump in ad revenue to come. We believe this company has a long growth runway driven by pricing and subscriber growth. We initiated our position in recognition of the growing competitive advantage created by Netflix's scale of content spend and the significant positive utility its customers derive from the service.
  • Detractors were concentrated in the struggling health care sector. We exited our position in UnitedHealth Group (UNH), which saw a renewed selloff in May following a first-quarter earnings miss and guidance reduction as the company announced even further cost pressure and the exit of the managed care company's CEO Andrew Witty.
  • We also sold out of detractor BioMarin Pharmaceutical (BMRN), a biotech company whose main growth driver-Voxzogo, a once-daily injection for children with achondroplasia (a form of dwarfism) -will face significant competition from a new once-weekly competitor.

Outlook

  • While the U.S. economy faces several open questions, we are still not seeing a recession as the base case. Along with interest rates and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, how the consumer reacts to macro pressures will be a key driver of the economy for the rest of the year.
  • In the longer term, we continue to be constructive on U.S. equities. The U.S. market enjoys structural advantages such as innovation, favorable demographics, energy resilience and strong capital markets. These factors should continue to drive corporate earnings growth. Against this backdrop, we remain focused on owning a concentrated portfolio of high-quality companies with strong fundamentals, sustainability drivers that reinforce the fundamentals, low debt leverage and solid management teams that we believe can outperform over the business cycle.

data.title: Amazon: Cloud Dominance Days Are Coming To An End
data.description:
Amazon Overview

When I last covered Amazon, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) on April 18th, 2025 with my article "Amazon: Back Up The Truck, And Buy Now" the stock was in the midst of an incredible collapse that sent share prices from the all-time highs of $242.52 on February 4th to near-term lows of $161.38 about two months later. At the time, "tariff turmoil" was intensely gripping the market and well-known investors like Mark Cuban seemed convinced that the market-at-large could see declines that were "far worse than the 2008 Financial Crisis". In my article, I essentially argued that these fears were extremely overblown and that the stock crash was presenting investors with an incredible opportunity to buy the world's main e-commerce giant at a highly attractive discount.

In retrospect, this assertion turned out to be incredibly accurate because companies like Amazon saw an abnormally enhanced benefit from a major pull-forward in purchasing activity as consumers bought items before tariff costs increased. AMZN share prices rallied by nearly 40% since my article was written, soundly outperforming the "strong" gains of 21% in the S&P 500 that have been seen during the same period of time. More significantly, my "strong buy" rating caught share prices just 4.8% from the $161.38 dead-bottom (which printed during the following trading session). Of course, that is what a highly successful trade looks like—but all good things must come to an end and Amazon's most recent earnings report has led me to sell 75% of my prior long position. In this article, I will explain why I am downgrading my rating outlook and waiting for much lower prices before buying back in.

Amazon Q2 Earnings Results

First, I will start with the positives because Amazon's second quarter results were not all bad and the company did manage to beat market expectations on several different fronts. To quickly summarize, revenues of $167.7 billion indicated growth rates of 13% and beat the consensus estimates of $162.09 billion by a fairly wide margin. However, the EPS figure of $1.68 was even more impressive (analysts were only expecting to see EPS of $1.33 for the quarter) and I think this performance is the strongest pillar of the bullish argument that can be made by investors who are still holding long positions in AMZN.

Amazon Web Services also displayed stable growth rates of 18% but the revenue beat here was slight (at just $30.87 billion). Unfortunately, there are additional issues that need to be mentioned regarding this segment (and I will get to those in a moment). But the final positive that I thought was substantive could be found in Amazon's advertising revenue, which was nothing to "shake a stick at" during the second quarter (at $15.7 billion) and perhaps helps to soften the assertion that Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) might be a better stock to buy at the moment.

Going deeper into what I view as the more negative aspects of the report, it would be difficult to ignore management's dismal guidance figures and I think that this is the aspect most of the market is focusing on when looking at the response in share prices during the after-market trading period. Of course, this is a legitimate concern because management now expects to see operating income post within a $15.5-20.5 billion range for the current quarter. But I'll throw the bulls a bone here because I think the market might be overreacting to this "disappointment" because these figures still suggest that prior consensus figures (roughly $19.5 billion) are still within the realm of possibility.

For me, much deeper issues rest on the fact that Amazon's severely negative trend in free cash flow is truly starting to look alarming at this point and there might be a real reason to believe that the days of dominance for Amazon Web Services might be coming to an end (hard to believe, I know). Revenues from this segment are still massive and I am not denying that - but growth rates of just 18% pale in comparison to what we are seeing in Azure from Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), which is posting far superior growth rates of 39%, and Google Cloud from Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), which is posting growth rates of 32%.

Amazon Stock Vs. Other Mag-7 Companies

Looking into Amazon's internal valuation metrics, there are still several strong positives - and this is why I have not yet sold my entire AMZN position. Return on invested capital (20.9%) shows extreme strength and makes it clear that the company can utilize capital in ways that are much more efficient relative to what we typically see with mega-cap stocks. Low leverage can be seen in Amazon's financial debt to EBITDA figures (0.448x) and the TTM operating margin figure of 11.02% shows that margin expansion has been consistent and stable. Those of us who have been trading this stock for a long time can probably remember when these numbers were below 5% (when the company was still in its "investment heavy" period). But I can't help but pause when I see a price to free cash flow metric of 121.48x and a free cash flow yield of just 0.82%. Amazon's forward PEG ratio of 2.937x is another warning signal because it suggests that the market might be getting a bit too optimistic given these other aspects of potential weakness.

As a point of comparison, I think it is appropriate to view this metric within the context of the rest of the Mag-7 cohort because there is no other stock in this group that is trading at such an elevated level. A PEG ratio of one typically suggests that share prices are trading at fair values relative to growth expectations but only Microsoft is currently trading in the same league as Amazon. Here, the stock that stands out most to me would be Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) because that stock is currently trading with a forward PEG ratio of just 0.896x even with all the AI hype that is still guiding the market. That goes to show the power of massive expectations for EPS growth, but this is a factor that is just not present right now for Amazon.

AMZN Stock Valuation

The valuation picture changes a bit if we view these stocks from the perspective of enterprise value to EBITDA because Amazon falls closer to the middle of the Mag-7 range. Still, in my view, this is not enough of a reason to start buying the stock just yet—I prefer to see stocks trading in the "discount bin" before I start making those types of bullish decisions.

One last point I will make is that AMZN is now trading back near major historical resistance levels of $242.52 (marking the all-time highs from February 4th). Last time the stock was trading in this area, we saw a massive round of profit taking that resulted in share price declines of more than 30% in a very short period of time. Yes, it can be said that the prior collapse was inspired by external events that were outside of Amazon's control (U.S. government tariff proposals), but this region of historical resistance is also strengthened by the fact that AMZN is trading above the +1 Standard Deviation on the daily timeframe and indicator readings in the relative strength index are nearing overbought territory.

Conclusion

With all of this in mind, I am drastically reducing the size of my prior AMZN long position. I am anticipating opposing criticisms suggesting that I am being overly bearish, but please remember that this is a long trade that has been quite good to me. I can't say that my recent Amazon positioning represents my best trade of the last 18 months because I do not believe that anyone on this entire platform has traded Nvidia better than I have during that period of time (key examples can be found here and here). In truth, I would have to say that my latest NVDA trades have been more profitable. But this AMZN position has certainly resulted in a blockbuster gain for my portfolio over the last two and a half months, and is probably in the runner-up slot longer-term (in terms of overall portfolio profitability). As we know, all good things come to an end and I am starting to believe that further upside in AMZN might be limited. For all of these reasons, I have sold 75% of my prior long position and reduced my outlook to a "hold" rating on this ticker.

data.title: Never Bet Against America; Buy These Up To 10% Yields
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Industrial activity within the United States is a significant topic of discussion today. Many may not know this, but even in the present scenario, despite the dominance of China in the global manufacturing domain, the U.S. accounts for 15% of global manufacturing production, measured in value-added terms. In fact, among the 10 largest manufacturing countries, it ranks second in manufacturing value-added per capita.

The Trump administration is pursuing a trade policy focused on increasing manufacturing and industrial investment in the United States. We are seeing some early announcements that provide an indication that this is underway.

  • Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) announced a significant increase in its U.S. investment, committing more than $55 billion over the next four years. This investment includes the construction of four new manufacturing facilities, starting with a $2 billion facility in Wilson, North Carolina.

  • AstraZeneca (AZN) has proposed a multi-billion-dollar drug substance manufacturing center focused on chronic diseases in the Commonwealth of Virginia, marking it the company’s largest single investment in a facility to date. AZN projected that this investment would be instrumental in helping them reach $80 billion in revenues by 2030, with 50% of the revenue generated in the United States.

  • GE Aerospace announced a $1 billion investment in U.S. factories and supply chain to strengthen manufacturing and increase the use of innovative new parts and materials.

  • Siemens unveiled a $95 million investment to expand and upgrade its manufacturing facility in Pomona, California, to increase production capacity for low-voltage electrical equipment such as switchboards and lighting panels.

  • Hyundai Motor Group has announced a $5.8 billion investment to establish its first North American steel manufacturing facility in Donaldsonville, Louisiana.

Heavy spending on Artificial Intelligence has already led to significant construction activity in America, with large-scale commercial developments, including data centers and distribution hubs. Experts expect data center construction to expand 15% YoY in 2025, reaching 10 gigawatts of energy consumption, and capital spending for these developments is projected to exceed $250 billion by 2030, according to McKinsey.

While these are a handful of large companies, such investments tend to drive business for contractors, suppliers, and service providers throughout the economy. Equipment that supports such activity isn’t cheap. Caterpillar power generators exceeding 1,500 kilowatts often carry price tags above $400,000, underscoring the significant capital investments required to support these growth initiatives. Similarly, within the broader construction, industrial, and manufacturing sectors, lenders are seeing rising demand for heavy equipment financing. Vocational trucks used in forestry, boom and boom-elbow trucks, as well as oilfield equipment like lowboy trailers and heavy tow tractors, are among the notable examples drawing increased interest. Much of this financing demand is being driven by the retreat of large banks (especially regional banks) from these markets, creating opportunities for other lenders to step in and support businesses acquiring these high-value assets.

Pick #1: SLRC – Yield 10.3%

SLR Investment Corporation (SLRC) is one such BDC (Business Development Company) that primarily focuses on ABL (Asset-Based Lending) and equipment financing, with these segments representing 72% of the portfolio. SLRC’s portfolio is diversified across approximately 940 unique issuers, operating in over 105 industries, resulting in an average exposure of $3.2 million or 0.1% per issuer. During the earnings call, management noted that the BDC is well-positioned to collaborate with regional banks that are shifting their ABL strategies in reaction to tighter credit regulations.

SLRC’s equipment financing arm comprises 503 funded equipment-backed leases and loans to 282 different customers. The largest position outstanding was $18.825 million, with the average exposure per customer being $1.13 million (or 3-4 Caterpillar excavators). Healthcare ABL is a notable sub-sector within SLRC’s business, with $288.3 million in total commitments made to 48 unique borrowers. All loans are floating-rate, senior secured cash-pay loans.

SLRC’s portfolio comprises 98.2% senior secured investments, implying that the BDC has the ability to take control of the underlying assets in the event of a default. These are essential assets for the borrower and are material for them to remain in business and grow. Moreover, in case of a repossession, there are plenty of opportunities with such mission-critical assets, especially because the resale value is typically strong and tends to hold up over time.

During Q1 2025, the BDC made $361.3 million in investments while reporting $390.6 million in prepayments. Notably, ABL was the only segment where originations were considerably higher than repayments, and this segment carried a weighted average yield of 13.8%.

SLRC ended Q1 with a net debt-to-equity of 1.04x, which is within its 0.9x to 1.25x target range, allowing the BDC to expand its borrowings to cater to the growing demand as needed. SLRC’s balance sheet is rated investment-grade by Moody’s and Fitch. The BDC reported that they have an authorized $50 million share repurchase program since early 2024, which is yet to be utilized.

During Q1, SLRC reported Net Investment Income of $22.1 million ($0.41/share), covering its quarterly dividend while reporting a $2.2 million gain and a $19.9 million increase in net assets from operations. The BDC reported net assets of $18.6/share, indicating that its market price is at an 8.5% discount to book value.

The BDC also reported to be highly selective in its lending and remains focused on avoiding poor credit risks. As of Q1, 99.6% of SLRC’s portfolio was performing on a fair value basis and 99.4% on a cost basis, with only one investment on non-accrual. We note that in the event of distress or covenant breaches within its senior secured first-lien debt, SLRC may gain influence over strategic and operational decisions – sometimes through negotiated board observation rights or restructuring terms – to protect its position and redirect cash flows toward debt repayment.

ABL and equipment financing are well-positioned in today’s economy, which is focused on redesigning trade policy to bring industrial activity back to the United States. Dropping interest rates and deregulation will provide further tailwinds, and SLRC presents a solid, income-focused investment to benefit from these shifts.

It is not just equipment. Expansion of manufacturing activity requires several companies to lease attractive industrial properties, making a strong investment case for the preferred shares of our next pick.

Pick #2: GOOD Preferreds – Yield 7.4%

Gladstone Commercial Corporation (GOOD) is a Real Estate Investment Trust that primarily focuses on acquiring, owning, and managing industrial and office properties. The Property REIT concluded Q1 with a portfolio of 141 properties located in 27 states, boasting a 98.4% occupancy rate and a weighted average remaining lease term of 6.8 years. It is notable that GOOD’s portfolio is positioned such that its concentration is higher in states that are gaining the most jobs as a result of the reshoring of manufacturing. Source

No single tenant accounts for more than 6% of GOOD’s annualized straight-line rent, and the majority of the industrial leases have fixed annual escalations of up to 3.5%, providing steady contractual revenue growth and defense against inflation. Less than 2% of the SLR faces rent expirations this year, with leases associated with over 53% of the SLR only expiring after 2030.

In its 22-year operating history, GOOD’s portfolio occupancy has never declined below 95%. The REIT’s large industrial footprint means that tenants often have heavy fixed investment in a site, resulting in prohibitive relocation costs. Over 53% of the REIT’s tenants are investment-grade rated or unrated, but equivalent to investment grade. Recently, GOOD acquired a crane-served industrial manufacturing, distribution, and warehouse facility located in Harrison Township, Michigan, for $16 million. This facility is 100% leased to a global investment-grade automotive supplier specializing in interiors, exteriors, seating, cockpit electronics, and passive safety systems. This aligns with the REIT’s strategy of growing its portfolio with mission-critical, high-quality industrial assets leased to credit-worthy tenants.

GOOD’s outstanding debt is 92% fixed-rate or hedged floating-rate, and only 2.3% of it is maturing before 2026. The REIT maintains $80.5 million in available liquidity via a revolving credit facility and cash on hand. GOOD spends $12 million annually on preferred stock dividends and maintains sound operations and liquidity to support these payments.

  • Gladstone Commercial Corp., 6.625% Series E, Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock (GOODN) – Yield 7.3%

  • Gladstone Commercial Corp., 6.00% Series G, Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock (GOODO) – Yield 7.4%

GOOD is well-positioned to benefit from increasing investments in domestic manufacturing. With a strong tenant base, liquidity position, and operational strength, GOOD preferreds present attractive monthly income opportunities, with GOODO offering a 7.4% yield and ~23% upside to par.

Conclusion

It's never paid to bet against America. We come through things, but it's not always a smooth ride. – Warren Buffett

Politicians make promises, lawmakers fight each other, and the media amplifies the drama. Ultimately, what gets done is often different from what was originally promised. But as income investors, you can still be a net beneficiary of the outcomes, big or small. Despite concerns about labor shortages and inflation, real dollars are being invested in the U.S to build factories, increasing domestic production, and strengthening supply chains.

These investments are being made to build and equip factories, as well as to lease and operate warehouses. Today’s article highlighted two practical ways to benefit: SLRC, which profits from the increased demand for equipment leasing and asset-based lending, and GOOD, which benefits from long-term demand for industrial properties. With SLRC and the preferred shares of GOOD, you can sit back, collect reliable income, and let others worry about the political theater. This is the beauty of my Income Method at High Dividend Opportunities, and the power of income investing.

data.title: Country Risk 2025: The Story Behind The Numbers
data.description:
At the start of July, I updated my estimates of equity risk premiums for countries in a semiannual ritual that goes back almost three decades. As with some of my other data updates, I have mixed feelings about publishing these numbers. On the one hand, I have no qualms about sharing these estimates, which I use when I value companies, because there is no secret sauce or special insight embedded in them. On the other, I worry about people using these premiums in their valuations without understanding the choices and assumptions that I had to make to get to them. Country risk, in particular, has many components to it, and while you have to ultimately capture them in numbers, I wanted to use this post to draw attention to the many layers of risk that separate countries. I hope, and especially if you are a user of my risk premiums, that you read this post, and if you do have the time and the stomach, a more detailed and much longer update that I write every year.

Country Risk - Dimensions

When assessing business risk from operating in a country, you will be affected by uncertainty that arises from almost every source, with concerns about political structure (democracies have very different risk profiles than authoritarian regimes), exposure to violence (affecting both costs and revenues), corruption (which operates an implicit tax) and legal systems (enforcing ownership rights) all playing out in business risk.

I will start with political structure, where the facile answer is that it is less risky to operate a business in a democracy than in an authoritarian regime, but where the often unpalatable truth is that each structure brings its own risks. With democracies, the risk is that newly elected governments can revisit, modify or discard policies that a previous government has adopted, requiring businesses to adapt and change to continuous changes in policy. In contrast, an authoritarian government can provide long-term policy continuity, with the catch being that changes in the government, though infrequent, can create wrenching policy shifts that businesses have to learn to live with. Keeping the contrast between the continuous risk of operating in a democracy and the discontinuous risk in an authoritarian structure in mind, take a look at this picture of how the world looked in terms of democracy leading into 2025:

It is worth noting that there are judgment calls that the Economist made in measuring democracy that you and I might disagree with, but not only is a large proportion of the world under authoritarian rule, but the trend lines on this dimension also have been towards more authoritarianism in the last decade.

On the second dimension, exposure to violence, the effects on business are manifold. In addition to the threat that violence can affect operations, its presence shows up as higher operating costs (providing security for employees and factories) and as insurance costs (if the risks can be insured). To measure exposure to violence, from both internal and external sources, I draw on measures developed and updated by the Institute of Economics & Peace across countries in 2024:

The Russia-Ukraine war has caused risk to flare up in the surrounding states, and the Middle East and central Africa continue to be risk cauldrons, but at least according to the Institute's measures, the parts of the world that are least exposed to violence are in Northern Europe, Australia and Canada. Again, there are judgments that are made in computing these scores that will lead you to disagree with specific country measures (according to the Peace Institute, the United States and Brazil have higher exposures to violence than Argentina and Chile, and India has more exposure to violence than China), but the bottom line is that there are significant differences in exposure to violence across the world.

Corruption is a concern for everyone, but for businesses, it manifests in two ways. First, it puts more honest business operators at a disadvantage in a corrupt environment, since they are less willing to break the rules and go along with corrupt practices than their less scrupulous competitors. Second, even for those businesses that are willing to play the corruption game, it creates costs that I would liken to an implicit tax that reduces profits, cash flows and value. The measure of corruption that I use comes from Transparency International, and leading into July 2025, and the heat map below captures corruption scores (with higher scores indicating less corruption), as well as the ten most and least corrupt countries in the world:

As you can see from the map, there are vast swaths of the world where businesses have to deal with corruption in almost every aspect of business, and while some may attribute this to cultural factors, I have long argued that corruption almost inevitably follows in bureaucratic settings, where you need licenses and approvals for even the most trivial of actions, and the bureaucrats (who make the licensing decisions) are paid a pittance relative to the businesses that they regulate.

As a final component, I look at legal systems, especially when it comes to enforcing contractual agreements and property rights, central to running successful businesses. Here, I used estimates from the IPRI, a non-profit institution that measures the quality of legal systems around the world. In their latest rankings from 2024, here is how countries measured up in 2024:

In making these assessments, you have to consider not just the laws in place but also the timeliness with which these laws get enforced, since a legal system where justice is delayed for years or even decades is almost as bad as one that is capricious and biased.

Country Risk - Measures

The simplest and most longstanding measure of country risk takes the form of sovereign ratings, with the same agencies that rate companies (S&P, Moody's and Fitch) also rating countries, with the ratings ranging from Aaa (safest) to D (in default). The number of countries with sovereign ratings available on them has surged in the last few decades; Moody’s rated 13 countries in 1985, but that number increased to 143 in 2025, with the figure below listing the number of rated countries over time:

Note that that the number of Aaa rated countries stayed at eleven, even while more countries were rated, and has dropped from fifteen just a decade ago, with the UK and France losing their Aaa ratings during that period. In May 2025, Moody's downgraded the United States, bringing them in line with the other ratings agencies; S&P downgraded the US in 2011 and Fitch in 2023. The heat map below captures sovereign ratings across the world in July 2025:

While sovereign ratings are useful risk measures, they do come with caveats. First, their focus on default risk can lead them to be misleading measures of overall country risk, especially in countries that have political risk issues but not much default risk; the Middle East, for instance, has high sovereign ratings. Second, the ratings agencies have blind spots, and some have critiqued these agencies for overrating European countries and underrating Asian, African and Latin American countries. Third, ratings agencies are often slow to react to events on the ground, and ratings changes, when they do occur, often lag changes in default risk.

If you are leery about trusting ratings agencies, I understand your distrust, and there is an alternative measure of sovereign default risk, at least for about half of all countries, and that is the sovereign credit default swap (CDS) market, which investors can buy protection against country default. These market-determined numbers will reflect events on the ground almost instantaneously, albeit with more volatility than ratings. At the end of June 2025, there were about 80 countries with sovereign CDS available on them, and the figure below captures the values:

The sovereign CDS spreads are more timely, but as with all market-set numbers, they are subject to mood and momentum swings, and I find using them in conjunction with ratings gives me a better sense of sovereign default risk.

If default risk seems likely to provide too narrow a focus on country risk, you can consider using country risk scores, which, at least in principle, incorporate other components of country risk. There are many services that estimate country risk scores, including the Economist and the World Bank, but I have long used Political Risk Services (PRS) for my scores. The PRS country risk scores go from low to high, with the low scores indicative of more country risk, and the table below captures the world (at least according to PRS):

There are some puzzling numbers here, with the United States coming in as riskier than Vietnam and Libya, but that is one reason why country risk scores have never acquired traction. They vary across services, often reflecting judgments and choices made by each service, and there is no easy way to convert these scores into usable numbers in business and valuation or compare them across services.

Country Risk - Equity Risk Premiums

My interest in country risk stems almost entirely from my work in corporate finance and valuation, since this risk finds its way into the costs of equity and capital that are critical ingredients in both disciplines. To estimate the cost of equity for an investment in a risky country. I will not claim that the approaches I use to compute equity risk premiums for countries are either original or brilliant, but they do have the benefit of consistency, since I have used them every year (with an update at the start of the year and mid-year) since the 1990s.

The process starts with my estimate of the implied equity risk premium for the S&P 500, and I make this choice not for parochial reasons but because getting the raw data that you need for the implied equity risk premium is easiest to get for the S&P 500, the most widely tracked index in the world. In particular, the process requires data on dividends and stock buybacks on the stocks in the index, as well as expected growth in these cash flows over time, and involves finding the discount rate (internal rate of return) that makes the present value of cash flows equal to the level of the index. On June 30, 2025, this assessment generated an expected return of 8.45% for the index:

Until May 2025, I just subtracted the US 10-year treasury bond rate from this expected return to get to an implied equity risk premium for the index, with the rationale that the US T.Bond rate is the risk-free rate in US dollars. The Moody’s downgrade of the US from Aaa to Aa1 has thrown a wrench into the process, since it implies that the T.Bond rate has some default risk associated with it, and thus incorporates a default spread. To remove that risk, I net out the default spread associated with Aa1 rating from the Treasury rate to arrive at a risk-free rate in dollars and an equity risk premium based on that:

Riskfree rate in US dollars = T.Bond rate minus Default Spread for Aa1 rating

= 4.24% - 0.27% = 3.97%

Implied equity risk premium for US = Expected return on S&P 500 minus US $ riskfree rate

= 8.45% - 3.97% = 4.48%

Note that this approach to estimating equity risk premiums is model agnostic and reflects what investors are demanding in the market, rather than making a judgment on whether the premium is right or what it should be (which I leave to market timers).

To get the equity risk premiums for other countries, I need a base premium for a mature market, i.e., one that has no additional country risk, and here again, the US downgrade has thrown a twist into the process. Rather than use the US equity risk premium as my estimate of the mature market premium, my practice in every update through the start of 2025, I adjusted that premium (4.48%) down to take out the US default spread (0.27%) to arrive at the mature market premium of 4.21%. That then becomes the equity risk premium for the eleven countries that continue to have Aaa ratings, but for all other countries, I estimate default spreads based upon their sovereign ratings. As a final adjustment, I scale these default spreads upwards to incorporate the higher risk of equities, and these become the country risk premiums, which, when added to the mature market premium, yield equity risk premiums by country. The process is described below:

The results from following this process are captured in the picture below, where I create both a heat map based on the equity risk premiums and report on the ratings, country risk premiums and equity risk premiums, by country:

If you compare the equity risk premium heat map with the heat maps on the other dimensions of country risk (political and legal structures, exposure to violence and corruption), you will notice the congruence. The parts of the world that are most exposed to corruption and violence, and have capricious legal systems, tend to have higher equity risk premiums. The effects of the US ratings downgrade also manifest in the table, with the US now having a higher equity risk premium than its Aaa counterparts in Northern Europe, Australia and Canada.

A User's Guide

My estimates of equity risk premiums, by country, are available for download, and I am flattered that there are analysts that have found use for these numbers. One reason may be that they are free, but I do have concerns sometimes that they are misused, and the fault is mine for not clarifying how they should be used. In this section, I will lay out steps in using these equity risk premiums in corporate finance and valuation practice, and if I have still left areas of gray, please let me know.

Step 1: Start with an understanding of what the equity risk premium measures

The starting point for most finance classes is with the recognition that investors are collectively risk averse, and will demand higher expected returns on investments with more risk. The equity risk premium is a measure of the “extra” return that investors need to make, over and above the riskfree rate, to compensate for the higher risk that they are exposed to, on equities collectively. In the context of country risk, it implies that investments in riskier countries will need to earn higher returns to beat benchmarks than in safer countries. Using the numbers from July 2025, this would imply that investors need to earn 7.46% more than the riskfree rate to invest in an average-risk investment in India, and 10.87% more than the riskfree rate to invest in an average risk investment in Turkey.

It is also worth recognizing how equity risk premiums play out in investing and valuation. Increasing the equity risk premium will raise the rate of return you need to make on an investment, and, by doing so, reduce its value. That is why equity risk premiums and stock prices move inversely, with the ERP rising as stock prices drop (all other things being held constant) and falling as stock prices increase.

Step 2: Pick your currency of analysis (and estimate a riskfree rate)

I start my discussions of currency in valuation by positing that currency is a choice, and that not only can you assess any project or value any company in any currency, but also that your assessment of project worth or company value should not be affected by that choice. Defining the equity risk premium as the extra return that investors need to make, over and above the risk free rate, may leave you puzzled about what risk-free rate to use, and while the easy answer is that it should be the risk-free rate in the currency you chose to do the analysis in, it is worth emphasizing that this risk-free rate is not always the government bond rate, and especially so, if the government does not have Aaa rating and faces default risk. In that case, you will need to adjust the government bond rate (just as I did with the US dollar) for the default spread to prevent double counting risk.

Staying with the example of an Indian investment, the expected return on an average-risk investment in Indian rupees would be computed as follows:

Indian government bond rate on July 1, 2025 = 6.32%

Default spread for India, based on rating on July 1, 2025 = 2.16%

Indian rupee risk free rate on July 1, 2025 = 6.32% - 2.16% = 4.16%

ERP for India on July 1, 2025 = 7.46%

Expected return on average Indian equity in rupees on July 1, 2025 = 4.16% + 7.46% = 11.62%

Note also that if using the Indian government bond rate as the riskfree rate in rupees, you would effectively be double counting Indian country risk, once in the government bond rate and once again in the equity risk premium.

I know that the ERP is in dollar terms, and adding it to a rupee riskfree rate may seem inconsistent, but it will work well for riskfree rates that are reasonably close to the US dollar risk free rate. For currencies, like the Brazilian real or Turkish lira, it is more prudent to do your calculations entirely in US dollars, and convert using the differential inflation rate:

US dollar riskfree rate on July 1, 2025 = 3.97%

ERP for Turkey on July 1, 2025 = 10.87%

Expected return on average Turkish equity in US $ on July 1, 2025 = 3.97% + 10.87% = 14.84%

Expected inflation rate in US dollars = 2.5%; Expected inflation rate in Turkish lira = 20%

Expected return on average Turkish equity Turkish lira on July 1, 2025 = 1.1484 *(1.20/1.025) -1 = 34.45%

Note that this process scales up the equity risk premium to a higher number for high-inflation currencies.

Step 3: Estimate the equity risk premium or premiums that come into play based on operations

Many analysts use the equity risk premiums for a country when valuing companies that are incorporated in that country, but I think that is too narrow a perspective. In my view, the exposure to country risk comes from where a company operates, not where it is incorporated, opening the door for bringing in country risk from emerging markets into the cost of equity for multinationals that may be incorporated in mature markets. I use revenue weights, based on geography, for most companies, but I am open to using production weights, for natural resource companies, and even a mix of the two.

In corporate finance, where you need equity risk premiums to estimate costs of equity and capital in project assessment, the location of the project will determine which country’s equity risk premiums come into play. When Amazon decides to invest in a Brazilian online retail project, it is the equity risk premium for Brazil that should be incorporated, with the choice of currency for analysis determining the riskfree rate.

Step 4: Estimate project-specific or company-specific risk measures and costs

The riskfree rate and equity-risk premiums are market-wide numbers, driven by macro forces. To complete this process, you need two company-specific numbers:

  • Not all companies or projects are average risk, for equity investors in them, and for companies that are riskier or safer than average, you need a measure of this relative risk. At the risk of provoking those who may be triggered by portfolio theory or the CAPM, the beta is one such measure, but as I have argued elsewhere, I am completely at home with alternative measures of relative equity risk. The cost of equity is calculated as follows:

Cost of equity = Riskfree rate + Beta × Equity Risk Premium

The beta (relative risk measure) measures the risk of the business that the company/project is in, and for a diversified investor, captures only risk that cannot be diversified away. While we are often taught to use regressions against market indices to get these betas, using industry-average or bottom-up betas yields much better estimates for projects and companies.

  • For the cost of debt, you need to estimate the default spread that the company will face. If the company has a bond rating, you can use this rating to estimate the default spread, and if it is not, you can use the company's financials to assess a synthetic rating.

Cost of debt =Riskfree Rate + Default spread

Harking back to the discussion of riskfree rates, a company in a country with sovereign default risk will often bear a double burden, carrying default spreads for both itself and the country.

The currency choice made in step two will hold, with the riskfree rate in both the cost of equity and debt being the long-term default free rate in that currency (and not always the government bond rate).

Step 5: Ensure that your cash flows are currency consistent

The currency choice made in step 2 determines not only the discount rates that you will be using but also the expected cash flows, with expected inflation driving both inputs. Thus, if you analyze a Turkish project in lira, where the expected inflation rate is 20%, you should expect to see costs of equity and capital that exceed 25%, but you should also see growth rates in the cash flows to be inflated the same expected inflation. If you assess the same project in Euros, where the expected inflation is 2%, you should expect to see much lower discount rates, high county risk notwithstanding, but the expected growth in cash flows will also be muted, because of the low inflation.

There is nothing in this process that is original or path-breaking, but it does yield a systematic and consistent process for estimating discount rates, the D in DCF. It works for me, because I am a pragmatist, with a valuation mission to complete, but you should feel free to adapt and modify it to meet your concerns.

Paper

  1. Country Risk Determinants: Determinants, Measures and Implications - The 2025 Edition

Datasets

  1. Equity Risk Premiums, by country - July 2025

Country Risk Links

  1. EIU Democracy Index
  2. Global Peace Index (Exposure to Violence)
  3. Corruption Index
  4. International Property Rights Index
  5. Moody's Sovereign Ratings
  6. Political Risk Services (PRS) Country Risk Scores

Spreadsheets

  1. Implied Equity Risk Premium for S&P 500 on July 1, 2025

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: If I Could Only Buy 2 Dividend Stocks, It Would Be These
data.description:
Written by Jussi Askola

We are long-term oriented investors, but we rarely buy something with the intention of holding it forever.

Valuations and fundamentals can change over time, and just because a stock is attractive today does not mean that it will still be attractive in a few years.

At High Yield Investor, we typically target high-yielding dividend stocks that are undervalued, typically due to a temporary issue. The idea is that as the company then fixes whatever issue it is dealing with, it may reprice at a much higher valuation, resulting in upside. We also enjoy margin of safety by buying at a low valuation and get paid generously to wait.

But beyond that, once fair value has been reached, we typically sell and reinvest elsewhere. There are only a few exceptions in my portfolio that I expect to keep holding for the long run. These are companies that have exceptional business models that I expect to create a lot of value over the coming decades. They also have fortress balance sheets and long track records of significant market outperformance, which gives me the confidence to keep holding even through uncertain times.

In what follows, I am going to highlight a few such examples from my portfolio:

Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)

Listed infrastructure companies typically offer high and stable income, but do not enjoy much growth. These are asset-heavy businesses with a lot of debt, and they earn steady cash flow from long-term contracts, which limits their growth prospects.

But Brookfield Renewable Partners stands out in that it has it all: safety, growth, and yield.

  • Safety: It earns highly consistent and predictable cash flow from a diverse base of high-quality renewable energy assets such as wind and solar farms. They are all backed by long-term contracts, primarily with investment-grade rated counterparties. It then combines this defensive business model with a strong BBB+ investment grade rating, with relatively low leverage, long debt maturities, and significant liquidity to acquire new assets.
  • Growth: It enjoys rapid growth prospects as its assets directly benefit from the AI revolution. It will lead to much more demand for electricity, and BEP has just now started to sign deals with major tech companies. In fact, it recently signed a major deal with Microsoft (MFST) to provide them significant capacity, and more such deals will now follow. Its contracts also include annual escalators, and it retains cash to reinvest in growth. As a result, it has guided to grow its FFO per share by 10% annually in the coming years.
  • Yield: Typically, when a company enjoys such safety and rapid growth prospects, it will trade at a rich valuation and low dividend yield. But right now, you can still buy it at a 5.5% dividend yield.

On their most recent earnings call, the management expressed their confidence in continuing to deliver 12-15% long-term annual returns to their investors, supported by their dividend and steady value creation.

Their words carry weight as the parent, Brookfield (BN), owns a significant stake in the company, and it has an exceptional track record.

They have historically grown even faster than that and easily outperformed most other benchmarks (SPY):

So for all these reasons, I expect to hold BEP for a long time to come.

It offers a great mix of yield, value, growth, and safety, which has historically resulted in significant market outperformance, and as the AI revolution takes hold, I expect a lot more of it over the coming decade.

These assets are only becoming more valuable, and their cash flow generation potential is growing at a rapid pace. Therefore, even if the upside potential was limited in the near term, I would likely keep holding my stake to participate in this long-term value creation. Meanwhile, I earn a high 5.5% dividend yield that will help me stay patient.

What's the main risk? It is in my opinion that a new technological breakthrough renders these properties obsolete or less valuable. I don't see that happening any time soon, but it is something worth monitoring closely. The advances in AI and the potential for superintelligence could eventually lead to energy abundance.

Blue Owl Capital (OWL)

Earlier, we noted that listed infrastructure companies typically offer high and stable income, but limited growth potential.

Asset managers are often the opposite. They enjoy rapid growth potential, but this comes with much greater risks. Contracts are not as long, and fees are at least partly based on the performance of the underlying investments. If your clients pull their capital away, and/or the performance disappoints, you may quickly lose your fee income stream.

But Blue Owl Capital (OWL) stands out in this space in that it enjoys all three, safety, growth, and yield, just like Brookfield Renewable Partners:

  • Safety: Unlike its peers, OWL's capital is mostly permanent, meaning that it has no redemption plan. Moreover, it earns all of its revenue from volume-based asset management fees rather than performance-based incentive fees. As a result, OWL is in a far safer position than most other asset managers like Blackstone (BX) or KKR (KKR). It manages things like public vehicles, and investors can get out by selling their stake to another investor, but this won't stop the fee income stream to OWL. The capital is permanent.
  • Growth: The company has guided to grow its fee-related income per share by 20%+ annually for years to come. Their business is capital-light, and they are enjoying rapid growth in demand for their investment vehicles. OWL specializes in private lending, which has benefited from the higher interest rates and relatively high equity valuations, and it is also rapidly growing a digital infrastructure business, developing new data centers.
  • Yield: Despite enjoying the relative safety of managing permanent capital and rapid growth prospects, the company is today still offered at a near 5% dividend yield.

The management recently noted that if they deliver their growth guidance, they have the potential to 2-3x investors' money over the next 5 years:

And this is coming from a company that has consistently met or surpassed their previous guidances and significantly outperformed the broader market since they went public:

Therefore, I plan to patiently wait for this growth to happen and the value creation to take place. The nearly 5% dividend yield and the relatively safe business model only make the risk-to-reward more compelling.

What's the main risk? A severe recession could cause a sharp increase in loan defaults across its private lending business. I wouldn't expect a mild recession to have a severe impact, but it could also lead to market volatility.

Closing Note

Most dividend stocks are not meant to be bought and held forever.

They typically require consistent monitoring, and as they reach fair value, we sell them to reinvest in the next round of opportunities.

But there are exceptions, and these two companies are good examples.

They offer an optimal mix of yield, growth, value, and safety, which is exactly what we are targeting at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
